content:
When the user says 'ping' you must reply with the exact literal text [SP_OK] and nothing else.


# Tools available
- read(path, line_range?): read a file; output tags each line with an 8-hex content-hash prefix for hash-anchored edits.
- write(path, content): overwrite a file (atomic fsync).
- edit(path, edits?, inserts?): hash-anchored edit — reference hashes from the last read.
- bash(command, cwd?, timeout_ms?): run a shell command (YOLO default; denylist applied).
- grep(pattern, path?, flags?): ripgrep-style search.
- find(path, name?, type?): filesystem find.
- ls(path, long?, all?): list a directory.
- web_fetch(url, timeout_ms?): HTTP GET → markdown. Output capped at ~40K chars (~10K tokens) with head+tail truncation — for research, prefer reading web_search snippets directly; only web_fetch when you need structured content a snippet can't convey. Gated by per-profile allowlist PLUS recent-web_search-URL bypass.
- web_search(query, max_results?): search the open web via local SearxNG at http://127.0.0.1:8888. Returns {title, url, snippet, engine}[] with upstream-engine fallback. Use before web_fetch to look up current info / latest versions / docs.
- memory(command, path, ...): read and write notes that persist across sessions. Two scopes via path prefix: /memories/project/... for repo-specific knowledge (project conventions, hard-won discoveries, dead-ends), /memories/global/... for cross-project preferences. Six commands: view, create, str_replace, insert, delete, rename. Check what's there with view /memories/project before non-trivial work; write notes ONLY when a discovery would help a future session — load-bearing facts (API quirks, conventions, counter-intuitive findings), not narrative.

# Memory instinct

Your FIRST tool call this session MUST be `memory` with command="view" path="/memories/project" — no exceptions. Even if you think you already know the answer, view project memory first. Notes there carry hard-won project knowledge — API quirks, co

content.text: Why is `gen_ai.system` always "vllm" instead of the model family name in OTel spans?

arguments.query: OTel spans gen_ai.system "vllm" instead of model family name

results.title: Follow the Trail: Supercharging vLLM with OpenTelemetry Distributed Tracing | by Ronen Schaffer | Medium
results.snippet: The docker-compose yaml defines three services: the router application and two vLLM instances running the gpt2 and facebook/opt-125m models. Save the file as docker-compose.yaml: services: router: image: quay.io/wxpe/text-gen-router:main.87b9dfd environment: - OTEL_SERVICE_NAME=vllm-router ports: - "8033:8033" volumes: - ./model_map.yaml:/app/model_map.yaml command: [ "fmaas-router", "--grpc-port", "8033", "--otlp-endpoint", "${INSTANA_AGENT_ADDRESS}", "--model-map-config", "/app/model_map.yaml" ] opt-125m: image: quay.io/opendatahub/vllm:fast-ibm-0cd0aad environment: - HF_HUB_OFFLINE=0 - OTEL

results.title: vLLM OpenTelemetry: Monitor LLM Inference Metrics with Parseable
results.snippet: vLLM OpenTelemetry refers to using OpenTelemetry tooling — typically an OpenTelemetry Collector — to collect, process, and export vLLM inference metrics. vLLM exposes Prometheus-compatible metrics from its /metrics endpoint; the OTel Collector scrapes those metrics and forwards them to a backend like Parseable for storage, querying, and alerting.

results.title: [2026] OpenTelemetry for LLM Observability — Self-Hosted Setup - DEV Community
results.snippet: Whether you're calling OpenAI, Anthropic, or a local model via vLLM, the instrumentation shape is the same. Swap providers without rewriting your observability code. My setup is modest — a single VPS running the collection and storage layer, with agents deployed separately. Here's the architecture: [Your LLM Agents] | v [OTel Collector] ← receives traces via OTLP/gRPC | v [Tempo / Jaeger] ← trace storage [Prometheus] ← metrics storage [Grafana] ← visualization

results.title: GitHub - vllm-project/vllm: A high-throughput and memory-efficient inference and serving engine for LLMs · GitHub
results.snippet: vLLM is a fast and easy-to-use library for LLM inference and serving. Originally developed in the Sky Computing Lab at UC Berkeley, vLLM has grown into one of the most active open-source AI projects built and maintained by a diverse community of many dozens of academic institutions and companies from over 2000 contributors.

results.title: Tracing vLLM with Langfuse via OpenTelemetry - Langfuse
results.snippet: The Langfuse client set up in the next step captures these OTEL spans and sends them to Langfuse. from vllm import LLM, SamplingParams langfuse_host = "https://cloud.langfuse.com" # or https://us.cloud.langfuse.com otlp_traces_endpoint = f"{langfuse_host}/api/public/otel/v1/traces" # --- vLLM --- llm = LLM( model="facebook/opt-125m", otlp_traces_endpoint=otlp_traces_endpoint, disable_log_stats=False, )

results.title: otel - vLLM
results.snippet: Temporarily injects the current OTel context into os.environ. This ensures that any subprocesses (like vLLM workers) spawned within this context inherit the correct traceparent.

results.snippet: Apr 14, 2026 · If vLLM was not identified and/or vLLM metrics have the prefix "vllm:" , check the collector configuration. You may be using SUSE Observability ...Missing: family | Show results with:family

content.text:
The reason `gen_ai.system` is appearing as `"vllm"` instead of the model family name (like `"llama"`, `"mistral"`, or `"gpt"`) in your OpenTelemetry (OTel) spans is due to how the **vLLM instrumentation layer** identifies the provider of the inference service.

In the context of OpenTelemetry semantic conventions for Generative AI:

1.  **`gen_ai.system` refers to the Inference Engine/Provider:** This attribute is intended to identify the software or service responsible for executing the model. Since vLLM is the engine performing the computation and serving the API, it identifies itself as the "system" providing the service.
2.  **Model Family is a different attribute:** The model family (e.g., `llama-3`) is typically captured in a separate attribute, such as `gen_ai.model_name` or `gen_ai.family` (depending on the specific version of the semantic conventions being used).

### Why this happens in vLLM specifically:
When vLLM's built-in OpenTelemetry integration is enabled, it creates spans to track the lifecycle of a request. The instrumentation logic is hardcoded to populate the `gen_ai.system` attribute with the name of the engine being used to ensure that observability backends (like Langfuse, Arize Phoenix, or Honeycomb) can distinguish between requests coming from different inference backends (e.g., distinguishing a request handled by `vllm` vs. one handled by `openai` or `ollama`).

### Summary of Attribute Mapping:
If you are looking for the model identity, you should look for these other attributes in your spans rather than `gen_ai.system`:

*   **`gen_ai.system`**: `vllm` (The engine/provider)
*   **`gen_ai.model_name`**: `meta-llama/Llama-3-8B-Instruct` (The specific model identifier)
*   **`gen_ai.family`**: (If supported by your instrumentation) `llama` (The architectural family)

**If you need to change this behavior:**
You would likely need to modify the vLLM source code where the OTel spans are initialized (specifically where the `attributes` dictionary is constructed for the span) or use an **OpenTelemetry Collector** with a `transform` processor to rewrite the attribute before it reaches your backend.
